Initial message:

Jason had never been a man of repetition. There was something suffocating about going in circles, doing the same thing over and over with no real sense of progress. But school… school was different. For the first time in what felt like forever, he’d found a rhythm that didn’t feel like a cage. It felt like movement. Like a life being rebuilt, slowly, with his own hands.

Getting a second shot at education—it stirred something in his chest he wasn’t used to letting breathe. Hope. A quiet, flickering thing that hadn’t burned in years. Maybe it was naïve, but it meant that his future wasn’t sealed shut, and that realization alone gave him enough reason to stick around.

He had a plan. Nothing extravagant…just enough structure to keep the chaos in check. Study during the day, knock a few skulls at night if needed, sneak back into the dorms before curfew, sleep, wake up, repeat. No distractions, no mess, no unnecessary people.

That plan started unraveling the moment the vice principal pulled him aside with a tight, bureaucratic smile.

“Since your… guardian was the last to finalize your dorm fees,” she began, her words tiptoeing around judgment, “we’re going to need to move you into a three students dorm. Rules are rules, I’m afraid. I hope you understand.”

Old bat geezer, can’t even be punctual about paying bills.

Jason grit his teeth behind a forced smile. He’d be damned if he ended up in some hellish room with strangers who left socks everywhere and didn’t know how to shut up at night.

But then the news came—Roy was one of the other roommates. That made things better. Roy was his best friend, his brother in arms. Living with Roy wasn’t a problem.

The real issue was the unknown. The third person. And that kept picking at his thoughts all day like a splinter he couldn’t dig out.

By the time classes ended, Jason’s head was swimming. He walked back to the dorms with Roy, half-listening as the redhead ranted about some professor who couldn’t pronounce “photosynthesis” properly. Jason’s eyes were on the pavement, his mind elsewhere. Who was this third guy? Loud? Quiet? Sloppy? Was he going to be some wide-eyed wannabe vigilante who’d ask Jason a million questions about Gotham? The thought alone made him want to bite someone.
